mainTopic: (continues) מצא גיטי נשים ושחרורי עבדים · סימנים בגט
overview: (1) The Mishnah states that found divorce and emancipation documents are not returned if the owner says 'give them'; the Gemara discusses Rav's ruling on returning documents found in court versus in the market, and the role of forgers (שטראי) and identical names. (2) Rabbi Yirmiya and Rav Ashi discuss the validity of signs (סימנים) on a document, such as witnesses testifying to a unique name or a specific hole, and Rav bar bar Chanah's uncertainty about the legal status of such signs. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Mishnah states that found divorce and emancipation documents are not returned if the owner says 'give them'; the Gemara discusses Rav's ruling on returning documents found in court versus in the market, and the role of forgers (שטראי) and identical names.

Rabbi Yirmiya and Rav Ashi discuss the validity of signs (סימנים) on a document, such as witnesses testifying to a unique name or a specific hole, and Rav bar bar Chanah's uncertainty about the legal status of such signs. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: רַבָּא's practical ruling in פּוּמְבֵּדִיתָא
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara relates that Rabba ruled to return a Get found in a flax house in Pumbedita.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא cites a practical עוֹבָדָא of רַבָּא. This is a setup line introducing a real-life case.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבָּא ruled to return a גֵּט found in a בֵּית כִּיתְנָא (flax house) in פּוּמְבֵּדִיתָא. This case is used to illustrate the application of the rules, though the exact details are disputed in the next lines.

phrases.aramaic: עֲבַד רַבָּה עוֹבָדָא בְּהָהוּא גִּיטָּא דְּאִשְׁתְּכַח
phrases.english: Rabba performed an action regarding that bill of divorce that was found
in a flax house in Pumbedita, when he heard it.

steps.triggerLanguage: עֲבַד רַבָּה עוֹבָדָא בְּהָהוּא גִּיטָּא דְּאִשְׁתְּכַח בֵּי כִיתָּנָא דְּפוּמְבְּדִיתָא כִּשְׁמַעְתֵּיהּ.

rashi.hebrew: כשמעתיה - דבעינן תרתי והכא חדא הוא דהואי ואכשריה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבא heard the ruling that two conditions are required for a found document to be returned, and since only one condition was present in this case, רבא nevertheless permitted the return.

steps.title: First interpretation: Flax market, no established identical names
steps.stepSummary: One opinion interprets the case as occurring in a flax market, where caravans are common but identical names were not established.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא presents the first interpretation of the עוֹבָדָא. This is a direct explanation of the previous case.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Some say בֵּית כִּיתְנָא refers to a place where מְזַבְּנִי כִּיתְנָא (flax is sold), which is a busy market where שְׁכִיחָן שְׁיָירָתָא (caravans are found). רַבָּא ruled to return it despite the caravan status because it was not הוּחְזְקוּ that two identical names existed there. This aligns with the refinement.

phrases.aramaic: אִיכָּא דְּאָמְרִי: הֵיכָא דִּמְזַבְּנִי כִּיתָּנָא וְהוּא שֶׁלֹּא הוּחְזְקוּ,
phrases.english: There are those who say: Where flax is sold and it was not established,
even though passing caravans are common.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיכָּא דְּאָמְרִי: הֵיכָא דִּמְזַבְּנִי כִּיתָּנָא וְהוּא שֶׁלֹּא הוּחְזְקוּ, אַף עַל גַּב דִּשְׁכִיחָן שְׁיָירָתָא.

rashi.hebrew: היכא דמזבני כיתנא - דשיירות מצויות שם לבא ולקנות ואהדריה משום דלא הוחזקו שני יוסף בן שמעון בעיר שנכתב בה ולא חיישינן לומר יש עיר אחרת ששמה כזו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that בית כיתנה is a market where caravans are commonly found, and רבא ruled to return the item because there was no חזקה that two people with the identical name יוסף בן שמעון lived in that specific city, so we do not suspect that the letter was sent to a different city with the same name.

steps.title: Second interpretation: Flax soaking place, established identical names but no caravans
steps.stepSummary: Another opinion interprets the case as occurring in a flax soaking place, where identical names were established but caravans were not common.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא presents the second interpretation of the עוֹבָדָא. This is a direct explanation of the previous case.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Others say בֵּית כִּיתְנָא refers to a place where תָּרוּ כִּיתְנָא (flax is soaked), which is not a busy market where לָא שְׁכִיחָא שְׁיָירוֹת (caravans are not found). רַבָּא ruled to return it even though הוּחְזְקוּ, because the lack of caravans removes the concern. This shows that the 'caravan' factor can override the 'established names' factor.

phrases.english: And there are those who say:

phrases.aramaic: הֵיכָא דְּתָרוּ כִּיתָּנָא וְאַף עַל גַּב דְּהוּחְזְקוּ,
phrases.english: Where they sell flax, and even though they are established,
That (passing caravans are not found).
phrases.notes: Refers to the rarity of travelers in that location.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְאִיכָּא דְּאָמְרִי: הֵיכָא דְּתָרוּ כִּיתָּנָא וְאַף עַל גַּב דְּהוּחְזְקוּ, דְּלָא (שְׁכִיחָא שְׁיָירוֹת).

rashi.hebrew: ואיכא דאמרי היכא דתרו כיתנא - במי המשרה שם נמצא הגט ומאי כשמעתיה שהוחזקו שני יוסף בן שמעון באותה עיר שנכתב בה ואכשריה דבמקום שנמצא בו לא היו שיירות מצויות:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that בית כיתנה refers to a place where flax is soaked in water, and the גט was found there, and the ruling to return it despite the established identical names is because caravans were not common in that location.

steps.title: רַבִּי זֵירָא's challenge from a בַּרִייתָא
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Zeira challenges the Mishnah with a Baraita that says a Get is returned if the husband admits, even after a long time.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבִּי זֵירָא brings a new challenge. This is a setup line introducing a parallel source.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי זֵירָא cites a בַּרִייתָא: if a גֵּט is found in the market and הַבַּעַל מוֹדֶה (the husband admits) to writing it, it is returned to the wife. This applies even after a long time (implied by the lack of 'immediately' in the בַּרִייתָא). This challenges the משנה in גִּטִּין that requires 'immediately'. רַבִּי זֵירָא resolves it using the same caravan distinction as רַבָּא.

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי זֵירָא רָמֵי מַתְנִיתִין אַבָּרַיְיתָא וּמְשַׁנֵּי.
phrases.english: Rabbi Zeira raises a contradiction between the Mishna and a Baraita, and he resolves it.
We learned: One who brings a bill of divorce and lost it,
if he found it immediately — it is valid,
and if not — it is invalid. And he raises a contradiction:
One who found a woman's bill of divorce in the marketplace,
when the husband admits — he returns it to the wife.
The husband does not admit —

phrases.aramaic: לֹא יַחְזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה.
phrases.english: he does not return it neither to this one nor to that one.
It teaches that at least when the husband admits —
he returns it to the wife, even after a long time!

steps.triggerLanguage: רַבִּי זֵירָא רָמֵי מַתְנִיתִין אַבָּרַיְיתָא וּמְשַׁנֵּי. תְּנַן: הַמֵּבִיא גֵּט וְאָבַד הֵימֶנּוּ, מְצָאוֹ לְאַלְתַּר – כָּשֵׁר, וְאִם לָאו – פָּסוּל. וּרְמִינְהִי: מָצָא גֵּט אִשָּׁה בַּשּׁוּק, בִּזְמַן שֶׁהַבַּעַל מוֹדֶה – יַחְזִיר לְאִשָּׁה. אֵין הַבַּעַל מוֹדֶה – לֹא יַחְזִיר לֹא לָזֶה וְלֹא לָזֶה. קָתָנֵי מִיהַת בִּזְמַן שֶׁהַבַּעַל מוֹדֶה – יַחְזִיר לָאִשָּׁה, וַאֲפִילּוּ לִזְמַן מְרוּבֶּה!

tosafot.hebrew: אין הבעל מודה לא יחזיר - אע"ג דנאמנת לומר גרשתני (גיטין דף סד:) הכא אינה נאמנת דמעיזה פניה כיון דהגט מסייעה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that although a woman is generally believed to claim her husband divorced her, she is not believed here because the Get in her possession supports her claim, making her testimony suspect due to potential bias.

tosafot.hebrew: ואפילו לזמן מרובה - תימה כיון שמודה שממנה נפל וכבר גירשה אמאי הוה לן למימר דלא יחזיר כיון דנאמן לומר גרשתיה כדאמר פרק יש נוחלין (ב"ב דף קלד:) אפילו נפל מאחר נחזיר לה לראיה בעלמא וי"ל דנאמן לומר גרשתיה ותהא מגורשת מכאן ולהבא הואיל ובידו לגרשה אבל הכא שאומר שכבר גרשה מזמן הכתוב בגט כמו שאינו נאמן למפרע כך אינו נאמן להבא ויחזיר לאשה דקתני היינו בעדים ותתגרש בו בחזרה זו ולא חיישינן שמא מאחר נפל ואפילו לזמן מרובה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses why the husband's admission that he already divorced her does not allow returning the Get for future effect, clarifying that his admission is only valid for future divorces he can still perform, not for retroactive effect, thus the Get is returned to the wife to be used with witnesses.

steps.title: רַבִּי זֵירָא's resolution: Caravan vs. non-caravan place
steps.stepSummary: Rabbi Zeira resolves the contradiction by distinguishing between a caravan place (Mishnah) and a non-caravan place (Baraita).
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא presents רַבִּי זֵירָא's resolution to the contradiction. He distinguishes between a caravan place and a non-caravan place.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבִּי זֵירָא applies the same distinction as רַבָּא. The מִשְׁנָה in גִּטִּין refers to a caravan place, while the בְּרֵיתָא refers to a non-caravan place. This shows רַבִּי זֵירָא agrees with רַבָּא's framework.

phrases.english: And they differ: here in a place where caravans are common,
and here in a place where caravans are not common.

steps.triggerLanguage: וּמְשַׁנֵּי: כָּאן בִּמְקוֹם שֶׁהַשְּׁיָירוֹת מְצוּיוֹת, וְכָאן בִּמְקוֹם שֶׁאֵין הַשְּׁיָירוֹת מְצוּיוֹת.

steps.title: Dispute on רַבִּי זֵירָא's view: Does he require established identical names?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara presents two opinions on whether Rabbi Zeira requires established identical names in a caravan place.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא clarifies רַבִּי זֵירָא's position on returning documents in a caravan place. There is a dispute about whether he requires הוּחְזְקו.
steps.deeperAnalysis: One opinion says רַבִּי זֵירָא agrees with רַבָּא: only if הוּחְזְקו. The other says he disagrees: even without הוּחְזְקו, we don't return in a caravan place. This sets up a question about why רַבִּי זֵירָא didn't use רַבָּא's resolution.

phrases.aramaic: אִיכָּא דְּאָמְרִי: וְהוּא שֶׁהוּחְזְקוּ דְּלָא נַהְדַּר, וְהַיְינוּ דְּרַבָּה.
phrases.english: There are those who say: And this is where it was established that it was not returned, and this is…
There are those who say:

phrases.aramaic: אַף עַל גַּב דְּלָא הוּחְזְקוּ לָא נַהְדַּר,
phrases.english: Even though it was not established that it was not returned,
And it disagrees with the opinion of Rabba.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִיכָּא דְּאָמְרִי: וְהוּא שֶׁהוּחְזְקוּ דְּלָא נַהְדַּר, וְהַיְינוּ דְּרַבָּה. אִיכָּא דְּאָמְרִי: אַף עַל גַּב דְּלָא הוּחְזְקוּ לָא נַהְדַּר, וּפְלִיגָא דְּרַבָּה.

steps.title: Question: Why didn't רַבִּי זֵירָא use רַבָּא's resolution?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks why Rabbi Zeira didn't use Rabba's resolution from the Mishnah in Gittin.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא asks a methodological question about רַבִּי זֵירָא's approach. It notes that רַבָּא didn't use רַבִּי זֵירָא's בְּרֵיתָא because a מִשְׁנָה is stronger than a בְּרֵיתָא.
steps.deeperAnalysis: But why didn't רַבִּי זֵירָא use רַבָּא's מִשְׁנָה resolution? This is a standard בִּשְׁלָמָא... אֶלָּא... structure. The question is about methodological preference.

phrases.aramaic: בִּשְׁלָמָא רַבָּה לָא אָמַר כְּרַבִּי זֵירָא,
phrases.english: Granted, Rabba did not say like Rabbi Zeira,
our Mishna is strong for him to raise a contradiction.

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא רַבִּי זֵירָא, מַאי טַעְמָא לָא אָמַר כְּרַבָּה?
phrases.english: But Rabbi Zeira, what reason did he not say like Rabba?

steps.triggerLanguage: בִּשְׁלָמָא רַבָּה לָא אָמַר כְּרַבִּי זֵירָא, מַתְנִיתִין אַלִּימָא לֵיהּ לְאַקְשׁוֹיֵי. אֶלָּא רַבִּי זֵירָא, מַאי טַעְמָא לָא אָמַר כְּרַבָּה?

rashi.hebrew: בשלמא רבה - דאמר שינויא ארומיא דמתניתין דגיטין אמתני' דנזיקין:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבה's resolution was based on a Mishnah in גיטין, which he applied to the Mishnah in נזיקין.

rashi.hebrew: לא אמר כר' זירא - דרמא מתני' אברייתא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that רבה did not follow רבי זירא's approach because he prioritized the Mishnah over the ברייתא.

rashi.hebrew: דמתניתין אלימא ליה לאקשויי - משום דהיא עיקר ויש תימה כשסותרות זו את זו וצריך לחזר שיתיישבו דאילו ברייתא יש לומר אינה עיקר רבי לא שנאה רבי חייא מניין לו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that a Mishnah is considered stronger for refutation because it is primary, whereas a ברייתא might be dismissed as not being the main tradition.

steps.title: Answer: The inference from the מִשְׁנָה is not definitive
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara answers that Rabbi Zeira could argue that the inference from the Mishnah is not definitive.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא provides the answer to the methodological question. רַבִּי זֵירָא could say the inference that תְּנוּ allows returning after a long time is not definitive.
steps.deeperAnalysis: Perhaps the מִשְׁנָה only means תְּנוּ allows returning immediately, as in גִּטִּין. Since the inference is weak, רַבִּי זֵירָא preferred to use the בְּרֵיתָא which is explicit.

phrases.english: He said to you: Who is teaching?
Here it says "give" –
They give, and even for a long time?
Perhaps here it says "give" –
They give, and forever as it stands for us to alter.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר לָךְ, מִי קָא תָנֵי: הָא אָמַר ״תְּנוּ״ – נוֹתְנִין וַאֲפִילּוּ לִזְמַן מְרוּבֶּה? דִּלְמָא הָא אָמַר ״תְּנוּ״ – נוֹתְנִין, וּלְעוֹלָם כִּדְקַיְימָא לַן לְאַלְתַּר.

rashi.hebrew: ולעולם כדקיימא לן כשנמצא לאלתר - ואם לאו פסול שמא אינו של זה אבל ברייתא ליכא לשנויי הכי דכיון דתנא בהדיא יחזיר לאשה הוה ליה לפלוגי בין לאלתר בין לזמן מרובה דלא דמיא למתניתין דהתם לא יחזיר תנא ומדוקיא הוה דייק יחזיר היכא דאמר תנו וכיון דלא תנא יחזיר לא הוה ליה לפלוגי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's answer applies only to the Mishnah, where the term תנו is ambiguous and could mean immediate return, but this answer cannot apply to the ברייתא because it explicitly uses the term יחזיר, which would require distinguishing between immediate and delayed return if the rule were limited to immediate return.

steps.title: Question: What is the basis of the dispute between רַבָּא and רַבִּי זֵירָא?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks what the basis of the dispute is between Rabba and Rabbi Zeira (according to the opinion that they disagree).
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא asks about the foundation of the dispute between רַבָּא and רַבִּי זֵירָא. According to the opinion that רַבִּי זֵירָא disagrees with רַבָּא, the גמרא asks what is the basis of their dispute.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This leads to the explanation of how they interpret the מִשְׁנָה about כׇּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵית דִּין. The question is about the foundation of the dispute.

phrases.aramaic: לְמַאן דְּאָמַר לְרַבִּי זֵירָא בִּמְקוֹם שֶׁהַשְּׁיָירוֹת מְצוּיוֹת,
phrases.english: According to the one who says that according to Rabbi Zeira, in a place where caravans are…

phrases.aramaic: וְאַף עַל גַּב שֶׁלֹּא הוּחְזְקוּ שְׁנֵי
phrases.english: and even though it was not established that two
Yosef ben Shimon, and he disagrees with Rabba,
With regard to what do they disagree?

steps.triggerLanguage: לְמַאן דְּאָמַר לְרַבִּי זֵירָא בִּמְקוֹם שֶׁהַשְּׁיָירוֹת מְצוּיוֹת, וְאַף עַל גַּב שֶׁלֹּא הוּחְזְקוּ שְׁנֵי יוֹסֵף בֶּן שִׁמְעוֹן, וּפְלִיגָא דְּרַבָּה, בְּמַאי קָא מִיפַּלְגִי?

steps.title: Answer: רַבָּא's view on כׇּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵית דִּין
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains that Rabba interprets 'כל מעשה בית דין' as referring to documents found in court, which are like caravan places.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא explains רַבָּא's position on the מִשְׁנָה. רַבָּא holds that כׇּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵית דִּין refers to documents found in court.
steps.deeperAnalysis: A court is like a caravan place (many people pass through). Therefore, in a court (caravan place), we only return if הוּחְזְקו is not established. This aligns with his ruling in רַב הוּנָא's court.

phrases.aramaic: רַבָּה סָבַר דְּקָתָנֵי כׇּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵּית
phrases.english: Rabba holds that any court enactment
the law is: this one returns,
that we are dealing with [a case] in court,
and a court is like a place where caravans are found,
and they were established – he does not return,
they were not established – he returns.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַבָּה סָבַר דְּקָתָנֵי כׇּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵּית דִּין הֲרֵי זֶה יַחְזִיר, דְּאִשְׁתְּכַח בְּבֵית דִּין עָסְקִינַן, וּבֵית דִּין כִּמְקוֹם שֶׁהַשְּׁיָירוֹת מְצוּיוֹת, וְהוּא שֶׁהוּחְזְקוּ – לֹא יַחְזִיר, לֹא הוּחְזְקוּ – יַחְזִיר.

steps.title: Answer: רַבִּי זֵירָא's view on כׇּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵית דִּין
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara explains that Rabbi Zeira interprets 'כל מעשה בית דין' as referring to documents found outside court.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא explains רַבִּי זֵירָא's position on the מִשְׁנָה. רַבִּי זֵירָא holds that כׇּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵית דִּין does not specify 'found in court'.
steps.deeperAnalysis: It refers to documents found outside court (אֲבָרַאי). Therefore, even in a caravan place, if הוּחְזְקו is not established, we return. This explains why he disagrees with רַבָּא.

phrases.english: And Rabbi Zeira says to you:
Does it teach "any enactment of the court
that was found in the court"?
"Any enactment of the court shall return" it teaches,
and forever that it was found outside.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְרַבִּי זֵירָא אָמַר לָךְ: מִי קָתָנֵי ״כׇּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵּית דִּין שֶׁנִּמְצְאוּ בְּבֵית דִּין״? ״כׇּל מַעֲשֵׂה בֵּית דִּין יַחְזִיר״ קָתָנֵי, וּלְעוֹלָם דְּאִשְׁתְּכַח אַבָּרַאי.

rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that אבראי refers to a place where caravans are not present, meaning the documents were found in a location without the usual commercial activity of a caravan.

steps.title: רַבִּי יִרְמְיָה's resolution: witnesses testify to a unique name
steps.stepSummary: רבי ירמיה resolves the contradiction by stating that the Mishnah refers to a case where the witnesses testify that they have never signed a גט for a יוסף בן שמעון other than this one.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא introduces רַבִּי יִרְמְיָה's alternative resolution to the earlier contradiction regarding returning a found גֵּט. רַבִּי יִרְמְיָה suggests the מִשְׁנָה's leniency applies only when witnesses explicitly state they have never signed another גֵּט for a person with the same name.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This eliminates the concern that the גֵּט belongs to a different couple with identical names. רַבִּי יִרְמְיָה's resolution hinges on the witnesses' testimony about the uniqueness of the name.

keyTerms.meaning: A specific name used as an example.

phrases.english: Rabbi Yirmeya said:
such as when the witnesses say:

phrases.aramaic: מֵעוֹלָם לֹא חָתַמְנוּ אֶלָּא עַל גֵּט
phrases.english: From of old we never signed except on a get
one of Yosef ben Shimon.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַבִּי יִרְמְיָה אָמַר, כְּגוֹן דְּקָא אָמְרִי עֵדִים: מֵעוֹלָם לֹא חָתַמְנוּ אֶלָּא עַל גֵּט אֶחָד שֶׁל יוֹסֵף בֶּן שִׁמְעוֹן.

rashi.hebrew: ר' ירמיה אמר - הא דקתני יחזיר כגון דאמרי עדים החתומין בו מעולם לא חתמנו כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that רבי ירמיה's resolution specifies that the Mishnah's leniency to return the גט applies only when the witnesses explicitly testify that they have never signed any other גט for a person with the same name.

tosafot.hebrew: מעולם לא חתמנו אלא על גט אחד כו' - ואומר הבעל שהוא אותו יוסף וא"ת והלא כשאין העדים אומרים כן אינו נאמן לומר שהוא שלו אלא אנו חוששין שמא מאחר נפל ועתה שעדים אומרים שלא חתמו אלא על גט אחד של יוסף בן שמעון למה יהא נאמן ליחוש שמא לאחר חתמו ולא לזה וי"ל דאף כי אין עדים מה שאינו נאמן אע"ג שאומר מכיר אני שהוא שלי לפי שמספק אומר שהוא שלו לפי שסבור שלא איבד אחר גט רק הוא לכך אומר שהוא שלו אע"פ שאינו מכיר אבל כשעדים אומרים שלא חתמו רק על גט אחד של יוסף בן שמעון במזיד לא ישקר אם חתמו לאחר לומר שלא חתמו דאינו חשוד לקלקלה כמו שנאמן לומר גרשתיה ואית ספרים דגרסי אלא על גט אחד של יוסף בן שמעון זה אתי שפיר טפי וצריך לומר שלא ראו עדים החתימה דאי ראו החתימה שהיא שלהם א"כ הוה ליה למימר מהו דתימא דלמא אתרמאי עדים כעדים וחתימה כחתימה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses the difficulty of why the husband is believed when witnesses testify they only signed one גט, whereas he is not believed without such testimony, explaining that without witnesses he might be guessing, but with witnesses he would not intentionally lie about their testimony since he is not suspected of corrupting the גט, and notes a variant text that strengthens this by specifying 'this' יוסף בן שמעון, implying the witnesses did not see the signature itself.

steps.title: Questioning the Mishnah's necessity
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא asks what the purpose of רבי ירמיה's statement is, since if the witnesses testify to the uniqueness of the name, it seems obvious that the גט should be returned.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא questions why the Mishnah explicitly states the גט should be returned when witnesses confirm the name's uniqueness. This is a setup line probing the text's redundancy.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The challenge assumes the return is self-evident if witnesses verify the name. It implies the Mishnah adds unnecessary detail unless addressing a hidden concern. The underlying assumption is that obvious cases don't require explicit teaching.

keyTerms.meaning: What is the purpose of stating?
That the name happened to be identical.

phrases.english: If so, what is there to say?

phrases.aramaic: מַהוּ דְּתֵימָא לֵיחוּשׁ דִּלְמָא אִתְרְמִי שְׁמָא כִּשְׁמָא,
phrases.english: What is it that you say, perhaps it happened that the name coincided with the name,
and witnesses with witnesses, it teaches us.

steps.triggerLanguage: אִי הָכִי, מַאי לְמֵימְרָא? מַהוּ דְּתֵימָא לֵיחוּשׁ דִּלְמָא אִתְרְמִי שְׁמָא כְּשְׁמָא, וְעֵדִים כְּעֵדִים, קָא מַשְׁמַע לַן.

steps.title: רַב אָשֵׁי's resolution: a specific hole as a sign
steps.stepSummary: רַב אָשֵׁי offers another resolution: the Mishnah refers to a case where the claimant specifies a hole next to a particular letter.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא introduces רַב אָשֵׁי's alternative resolution, focusing on a specific physical sign (a hole) on the גט. This is a new explanatory proposal.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב אָשֵׁי suggests the Mishnah's leniency applies when the claimant points to a unique physical feature, like a hole next to a specific letter. This provides a concrete basis for returning the גט. It shifts the focus from name uniqueness to physical verification.

phrases.english: Rav Ashi says:
Such as when he says:

phrases.aramaic: נֶקֶב יֵשׁ בּוֹ בְּצַד אוֹת פְּלוֹנִית.
phrases.english: There is a hole in it next to such-and-such a letter.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַב אָשֵׁי אָמַר, כְּגוֹן דְּקָא אָמַר: נֶקֶב יֵשׁ בּוֹ בְּצַד אוֹת פְּלוֹנִית.

steps.title: The necessity of specifying the hole's location
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא explains that רַב אָשֵׁי requires the hole to be next to a specific letter, not just any hole.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא clarifies that רַב אָשֵׁי's resolution requires the hole to be in a specific location. This is a clarifying explanation.
steps.deeperAnalysis: A general hole is insufficient; the claimant must specify its location relative to a letter. This ensures the sign is unique and verifiable. It tightens the criteria for valid identification.

phrases.english: And specifically next to such-and-such a letter,
But a hole in the world – no.
phrases.notes: A hole alone, without specifying its location, is not a valid distinguishing mark.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְדַוְקָא בְּצַד אוֹת פְּלוֹנִית, אֲבָל נֶקֶב בְּעָלְמָא – לָא.

rashi.hebrew: ודוקא בצד אות פלונית - דהוה ליה סימן מובהק ואפילו אם תימצי לומר גבי חזרת אבידה בסימנין דאיבעיא לן לקמן (בבא מציעא דף כז.) דאורייתא או דרבנן אפילו פשטת סימנין דרבנן יש לסמוך על סימן מובהק כזה למשרי אשת איש דאורייתא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that specifying the hole's location next to a particular letter constitutes a סימן מובהק, which is so reliable that even if one holds that returning a lost item based on signs is only a rabbinic obligation, this level of certainty is sufficient to permit a woman to remarry based on the husband's death, a matter of biblical law.

steps.title: רַב אָשֵׁי's uncertainty about the status of signs
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא states that רַב אָשֵׁי is uncertain whether signs are valid by Torah law or rabbinic law.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא introduces רַב אָשֵׁי's uncertainty regarding the legal status of signs. This is a new explanatory layer.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This uncertainty explains why רַב אָשֵׁי requires a specific sign: if signs are only rabbinically valid, a stricter standard is needed. If Torah-valid, a looser standard might suffice. It links stringency to legal classification.

phrases.aramaic: רַב אָשֵׁי מְסַפְּקָא לֵיהּ סִימָנִים אִי
phrases.english: Rav Ashi is uncertain whether distinguishing marks
of Torah law or of rabbinic law.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַב אָשֵׁי מְסַפְּקָא לֵיהּ סִימָנִים אִי דְּאוֹרַיְתָא אִי דְּרַבָּנַן.

rashi.hebrew: מספקא ליה לרב אשי סימנין - שמחזירין בהן אבידה אי דאורייתא היא ואפילו באיסור דאורייתא סמכינן אסימן כל דהו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רַב אָשֵׁי's uncertainty is whether סימנים for returning a lost object are דאורייתא, in which case we rely on any sign even for an איסור דאורייתא.

rashi.hebrew: אי דרבנן - ודוקא באבידה שהפקר ב"ד הפקר אבל במידי דאיסורא לא סמכינן ואי קשיא נימא הכי קסבר סימנין דרבנן ונפשוט בעיין דלקמן לאו פירכא היא דמנלן דפשיטא ליה אי נמי מספקא ליה אית עליה למיסר מספיקא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that if סימנים are דרבנן, they only apply to a lost object where בית דין has the power to declare it הפקר, but not for איסור, and addresses a potential objection by noting that even if רַב אָשֵׁי holds they are דרבנן, he might still require a stricter standard due to his uncertainty.

steps.title: רַבָּה בַּר בַּר חָנָה's lost גט
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא relates that רַבָּה בַּר בַּר חָנָה lost a גט in the study hall and was able to reclaim it using signs or recognition.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא introduces a case involving רַבָּה בַּר בַּר חָנָה to illustrate practical application of signs and recognition. This is a narrative example.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This case demonstrates how signs and recognition reclaim a lost גט. רַבָּה בַּר בַּר חָנָה's uncertainty highlights practical implications of the earlier discussion. It bridges theory and real-world application.

phrases.aramaic: רַבָּה בַּר בַּר חָנָה אִירְכַס לֵיהּ
phrases.english: Rabba bar bar Ḥana was asked
A bill of divorce in the study hall. He said:
If a distinguishing mark –
I have in it,
If the natural appearance of the eye –
I have in it.
They returned it to him. He said:

phrases.aramaic: לָא יָדַעְנָא אִי מִשּׁוּם סִימָנָא אַהְדְּרוּהּ נִיהֲלִי,
phrases.english: I do not know if because of a distinguishing mark they returned it to me,
And you think distinguishing marks are biblical.

phrases.aramaic: אִי מִשּׁוּם טְבִיעוּת עֵינָא אַהְדְּרוּהּ נִיהֲלִי,
phrases.english: If because of the natural appearance of the eye they returned it to me,
And specifically a clear-cut distinguishing mark is rabbinic.
But a person of the world – no.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַבָּה בַּר בַּר חָנָה אִירְכַס לֵיהּ גִּטָּא בֵּי מִדְרְשָׁא. אֲמַר: אִי סִימָנָא – אִית לִי בְּגַוְּיֵיהּ, אִי טְבִיעוּת עֵינָא – אִית לִי בְּגַוְּיֵיהּ. אַהְדְּרוּהּ נִיהֲלֵיהּ. אֲמַר: לָא יָדַעְנָא אִי מִשּׁוּם סִימָנָא אַהְדְּרוּהּ נִיהֲלִי, וְקָא סָבְרִי סִימָנִין דְּאוֹרַיְתָא. אִי מִשּׁוּם טְבִיעוּת עֵינָא אַהְדְּרוּהּ נִיהֲלִי, וְדַוְקָא צוּרְבָּא מִדְּרַבָּנַן. אֲבָל אִנְשָׁא דְּעָלְמָא – לָא.
